era_context: The mid-14th century, dominated by the Black Death pandemic (1347–1353), which killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe's population and caused severe mortality across Asia, North Africa, and the Middle East. The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in its early phase. The Yuan Dynasty in China was in terminal decline, facing peasant rebellions that would lead to its fall in 1368. Islamic sultanates in the Middle East and North Africa were experiencing demographic collapse and political instability.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa: limited contemporary written sources; plague impact poorly documented
Southeast Asia: sparse European records; local chronicles may exist but are underrepresented in Western historiography
Oceania and Pacific: no known written records for this period

events.title: Flagellant Movement peaks in Europe
events.region: Central and Western Europe (Holy Roman Empire, France, Low Countries)
events.description: The Flagellant movement—penitential processions of self-mortifying pilgrims—reached its peak intensity in 1349, spreading through German cities, the Low Countries, and France. Participants believed self-inflicted suffering would appease divine wrath and end the plague. The movement was eventually condemned by Pope Clement VI in October 1349 as heretical and disruptive to ecclesiastical authority. The phenomenon reflected mass psychological trauma and the breakdown of institutional religious authority in the face of catastrophic mortality.

sources.name: Jean de Venette, Chronicle (near-contemporary, written mid-14th century)
Papal bull of October 1349 condemning flagellants

events.certainty_note: Papal condemnation and multiple near-contemporary chronicles confirm the movement's peak in 1349. Dating is secure.

events.title: Persecution of Jews blamed for plague in Europe
events.region: Holy Roman Empire, France, Low Countries, Spain
events.description: Widespread massacres of Jewish communities occurred across Europe in 1349, driven by the false accusation that Jews had poisoned wells to cause the plague. Major pogroms occurred in Mainz, Cologne, Strasbourg, and other cities. Thousands were killed; entire communities were destroyed. These attacks were enabled by Christian authorities and sometimes actively encouraged by local rulers seeking to confiscate Jewish property and eliminate debt obligations. The violence was not spontaneous but often organized by civic authorities and clergy.
key_figures: Civic authorities in Strasbourg and Cologne

sources.name: Menorat ha-Maor (Jewish chronicle, contemporary witness accounts)
Strasbourg city records and Jewish community records

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary Jewish and Christian sources document the 1349 pogroms with specific dates and locations. The scale and coordination are well-attested.

events.title: Peasant rebellion in Languedoc (Pastoureau uprising)
events.description: A major uprising of peasants and shepherds (pastouraux) occurred in southern France in 1349, partly driven by plague-induced social breakdown and partly by anti-Jewish violence. The movement was initially directed against the nobility and clergy but became entangled with anti-Jewish pogroms. The uprising was suppressed by royal forces and local nobility by mid-1349, but it demonstrated the fragility of feudal order under plague conditions.
events.certainty_note: The uprising is attested in near-contemporary chronicles, but details are sparse and sometimes conflated with the 1320 Pastoureau movement. Dating to 1349 is accepted by modern scholarship but with some uncertainty about exact chronology.

events.title: Continued plague mortality and social collapse in Mediterranean
events.region: Mediterranean basin (Italy, Greece, North Africa, Levant)
events.description: The Black Death continued to devastate Mediterranean populations in 1349, with mortality rates in some cities reaching 50–70%. Venice, Genoa, and other trading hubs experienced severe depopulation. Agricultural production collapsed in many regions. Labor shortages began to create upward pressure on wages for surviving workers. The plague's impact on Mediterranean trade networks was profound, disrupting the commercial systems that had sustained medieval prosperity.

sources.name: Giovanni Villani, Nuova Cronica (Florentine chronicle, near-contemporary)
Matteo Villani, Chronicle (continuation of Giovanni's work)
Historiographic consensus from modern epidemiological and demographic studies

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources document continued plague mortality. Modern demographic reconstruction confirms the scale of depopulation.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces major peasant rebellions in China
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Toghon Temür (Togto Khan) faced escalating peasant rebellions in 1349, particularly in central and southern China. The Red Turban Rebellion, which would eventually topple the Yuan, was beginning to organize. These uprisings were driven by Mongol misrule, heavy taxation, and the ecological disruption caused by the plague and earlier flooding of the Yellow River. The rebellions signaled the beginning of the end for Mongol rule in China.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang (future Ming founder, early rebel leader)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, compiled earlier but covering this period)
Yuan Shi (Official Yuan Dynasty history, compiled by Ming)

events.certainty_note: The general timeline of Yuan rebellions in the late 1340s–1350s is well-established, but precise dating of specific uprisings in 1349 is less certain. Modern scholarship places the intensification of rebellions in 1348–1350.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English campaigns in Aquitaine
events.description: English forces under Edward III continued military operations in Aquitaine in 1349, maintaining English territorial control in southwestern France. The campaign was part of the broader Hundred Years' War but was limited in scope due to plague disruptions and resource constraints. The war itself was in a phase of reduced intensity compared to the 1340s, partly due to the pandemic's impact on military mobilization.
events.certainty_note: English military activity in Aquitaine in 1349 is attested in chronicles, but detailed accounts are sparse. The year was not a major campaign year compared to 1346–1347.

disconfirming_evidence: The Flagellant movement's role in combating the plague is sometimes romanticized as a form of 'proto-scientific' response; in fact, it was entirely ineffective and may have accelerated transmission through mass gatherings. The movement's condemnation by the papacy in 1349 is sometimes portrayed as institutional rigidity, but the Church's concern about heresy and social disorder was not entirely unfounded—the movement did challenge ecclesiastical authority and occasionally turned violent. The Jewish persecution narrative sometimes presents Jews as passive victims; while this is largely accurate, some Jewish communities did attempt armed resistance, though unsuccessfully. The scale of the Pastoureau uprising in 1349 is sometimes conflated with the larger 1320 movement; modern scholarship treats them as distinct events, though sources are ambiguous.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1349 is heavily weighted toward Western European sources, particularly Italian and French chronicles. The plague's impact on Islamic lands, sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia is poorly documented in surviving Western sources. Chinese sources are more abundant but were compiled after the Yuan's fall, introducing potential bias. The Jewish persecution narrative relies heavily on Jewish community records and later compilations, which may emphasize certain incidents over others. Plague mortality figures are estimates based on demographic reconstruction rather than contemporary counts. The Hundred Years' War is well-documented from English and French perspectives but less so from the perspective of Aquitanian or Gascon populations. Overall, 1349 is a year of moderate documentation, with strong evidence for European events but significant gaps for non-European regions.

graph_edges.note: Flagellant movement was a direct response to Black Death arrival
Jewish persecutions and Flagellant movement occurred simultaneously, both driven by plague panic
Pastoureau uprising overlapped with anti-Jewish violence
Continued plague mortality was part of the broader Black Death pandemic
1349 rebellions escalated into the formal Red Turban Rebellion of 1351
Aquitaine campaigns were part of the ongoing Hundred Years' War
